points: 1 lakh = 1,00,000 (5 zeroes); 1 crore = 1,00,00,000 (7 zeroes) = 100 lakhs; 1 arab = 1,00,00,00,000 (9 zeroes) = 100 crores = 1 billion; 1 million = 10 lakhs.

The Indian system groups digits from right to left in a 3-2-2-2… pattern using commas (thousands, lakhs, crores); the American (International) system uses a uniform 3-3-3… pattern (thousands, millions, billions).

To read and write large numbers easily, place commas correctly first, then translate group by group — e.g., 9,87,65,01,234 reads as '9 arab 87 crore 65 lakh 1 thousand 234' in the Indian system and '9 billion 876 million 501 thousand 234' in the American system.

Any number can be expressed in expanded form as a sum of place-value parts (e.g., 5,072 = (5 × 1000) + (7 × 10) + (2 × 1)); the minimum number of button-presses to form a number equals the sum of its digits.

Rounding up (approximated value > actual) is used when sufficiency matters; rounding down (approximated value < actual) is used when staying within a limit; the nearest neighbour at a given place is found by checking the digit immediately to the right — round up if ≥ 5, round down if < 5.

Very large numbers are best compared against familiar quantities to develop an intuitive sense of their size — e.g., 1 lakh people in a line spans 38 km; at 100 km/day it would take more than 10 years to travel even to the Moon (3,84,400 km).

Multiplying by 5 is the same as dividing by 2 and multiplying by 10 (since 5 = 10 ÷ 2); similarly, ×25 = ÷4 × 100, ×125 = ÷8 × 1000 — regrouping factors this way makes large multiplications much simpler.
The product of an m-digit and an n-digit number always has either (m + n − 1) or (m + n) digits; for example, the product of any two 2-digit numbers is always a 3- or 4-digit number.

Large-number problems can be solved step-by-step by breaking them into stages — first compute per minute, then per hour, then per day, then compare — making problems like 'Can 1 lakh buses hold Mumbai's population?' easy to answer without complex arithmetic.
